title: Tools for Cleaning Rectangular Data
text: dependency-free collection simple functions cleaning rectangular data. package allows detect, count replace values discard rows/columns using predicate function. addition, provides tools check conditions return informative error messages.
code: To cite arkhe in publications use:    Frerebeau N (2023). _arkhe: Tools for Cleaning Rectangular Data_.   Université Bordeaux Montaigne, Pessac, France.   doi:10.5281/zenodo.3526659 <https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.3526659>,   R package version 1.3.0, <https://packages.tesselle.org/arkhe/>.  Une entrée BibTeX pour les utilisateurs LaTeX est    @Manual{,     author = {Nicolas Frerebeau},     title = {{arkhe: Tools for Cleaning Rectangular Data}},     year = {2023},     organization = {Université Bordeaux Montaigne},     address = {Pessac, France},     note = {R package version 1.3.0},     url = {https://packages.tesselle.org/arkhe/},     doi = {10.5281/zenodo.3526659},   }  This package is a part of the tesselle project <https://www.tesselle.org>.

title: Tools for Cleaning Rectangular Data
text: can install released version arkhe CRAN : development version GitHub :
code: install.packages("arkhe") # install.packages("remotes") remotes::install_github("tesselle/arkhe")

title: Tools for Cleaning Rectangular Data
code: ## Load the package library(arkhe)  ## Create a matrix X <- matrix(sample(1:10, 25, TRUE), nrow = 5, ncol = 5)  ## Add NA k <- sample(1:25, 3, FALSE) X[k] <- NA X #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,]    5    8   NA    3    5 #> [2,]    9    7    8    4    2 #> [3,]    4   10    7   NA    2 #> [4,]   NA    5    5    4    3 #> [5,]    7    9    9    8    1  ## Count missing values in rows count(X, f = is.na, margin = 1) #> [1] 1 0 1 1 0 ## Count non-missing values in columns count(X, f = is.na, margin = 2, negate = TRUE) #> V1 V2 V3 V4 V5  #>  4  5  4  4  5  ## Find row with NA detect(X, f = is.na, margin = 1) #> [1]  TRUE FALSE  TRUE  TRUE FALSE ## Find column without any NA detect(X, f = is.na, margin = 2, negate = TRUE, all = TRUE) #>    V1    V2    V3    V4    V5  #> FALSE  TRUE FALSE FALSE  TRUE  ## Remove row with any NA discard(X, f = is.na, margin = 1, all = FALSE) #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,]    9    7    8    4    2 #> [2,]    7    9    9    8    1 ## Remove column with any NA discard(X, f = is.na, margin = 2, all = FALSE) #>      [,1] [,2] #> [1,]    8    5 #> [2,]    7    2 #> [3,]   10    2 #> [4,]    5    3 #> [5,]    9    1  ## Replace NA with zeros replace_NA(X, value = 0) #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,]    5    8    0    3    5 #> [2,]    9    7    8    4    2 #> [3,]    4   10    7    0    2 #> [4,]    0    5    5    4    3 #> [5,]    7    9    9    8    1

title: Tools for Cleaning Rectangular Data

what: Convert Row Names to an Explicit Column — append
title: Convert Row Names to an Explicit Column — append
text: Convert Row Names Explicit Column

title: Convert Row Names to an Explicit Column — append
code: append_rownames(x, ...)  # S4 method for data.frame append_rownames(x, after = 0, remove = TRUE, var = "rownames")

title: Convert Row Names to an Explicit Column — append
text: x data.frame. ... Currently used. length-one numeric vector specifying subscript, row names appended. remove logical scalar: row names removed? var character string giving name column use row names.

title: Convert Row Names to an Explicit Column — append
text: data.frame.

title: Convert Row Names to an Explicit Column — append
text: N. Frerebeau

title: Convert Row Names to an Explicit Column — append
code: X <- data.frame(   x = 1:5,   y = 6:10,   z = LETTERS[1:5] )  ## Assign column to row names (Y <- assign_rownames(X, 3)) #>   x  y #> A 1  6 #> B 2  7 #> C 3  8 #> D 4  9 #> E 5 10  ## Append row names to data.frame (Z <- append_rownames(Y)) #>   rownames x  y #> 1        A 1  6 #> 2        B 2  7 #> 3        C 3  8 #> 4        D 4  9 #> 5        E 5 10

what: arkhe: Tools for Cleaning Rectangular Data — arkhe-package
title: arkhe: Tools for Cleaning Rectangular Data — arkhe-package
text: dependency-free collection simple functions cleaning rectangular data. package allows detect, count replace values discard rows/columns using predicate function. addition, provides tools check conditions return informative error messages.

title: arkhe: Tools for Cleaning Rectangular Data — arkhe-package
text: arkhe uses following options() configure behavior: arkhe.verbose: logical scalar. R report extra information progress? Defaults TRUE.

title: arkhe: Tools for Cleaning Rectangular Data — arkhe-package

title: Assign a Specific Row/Column to the Column/Row Names — assign
code: assign_colnames(x, ...)  assign_rownames(x, ...)  # S4 method for data.frame assign_rownames(x, column, remove = TRUE)  # S4 method for data.frame assign_colnames(x, row, remove = TRUE)

title: Assign a Specific Row/Column to the Column/Row Names — assign
text: x data.frame. ... Currently used. column length-one numeric vector specifying column number become row names. remove logical scalar: specified row/column removed making column/row names? row length-one numeric vector specifying row number become column names.

title: Assign a Specific Row/Column to the Column/Row Names — assign
text: data.frame.

title: Assign a Specific Row/Column to the Column/Row Names — assign
text: N. Frerebeau

title: Assign a Specific Row/Column to the Column/Row Names — assign
code: X <- data.frame(   x = 1:5,   y = 6:10,   z = LETTERS[1:5] )  ## Assign column to row names (Y <- assign_rownames(X, 3)) #>   x  y #> A 1  6 #> B 2  7 #> C 3  8 #> D 4  9 #> E 5 10  ## Append row names to data.frame (Z <- append_rownames(Y)) #>   rownames x  y #> 1        A 1  6 #> 2        B 2  7 #> 3        C 3  8 #> 4        D 4  9 #> 5        E 5 10

title: Bootstrap Estimation — bootstrap
text: Samples randomly elements object replacement.

title: Bootstrap Estimation — bootstrap
code: bootstrap(object, ...)  # S4 method for numeric bootstrap(object, do, n, ..., f = NULL)

title: Bootstrap Estimation — bootstrap
text: object numeric vector. ... Extra arguments passed . function takes object argument returns single numeric value. n non-negative integer giving number bootstrap replications. f function takes single numeric vector (result ) argument.

title: Bootstrap Estimation — bootstrap
text: f NULL (default), bootstrap() returns named numeric vector following elements: original observed value applied object. mean bootstrap estimate mean . bias bootstrap estimate bias . error bootstrap estimate standard error . f function, bootstrap() returns result f applied n values .

title: Bootstrap Estimation — bootstrap
text: N. Frerebeau

title: Remove Empty Rows/Columns — compact
text: Removes empty rows/columns array-like object using predicate function.

title: Remove Empty Rows/Columns — compact
code: compact(x, ...)  compact_cols(x, ...)  compact_rows(x, ...)  # S4 method for ANY compact(x, margin = 1)  # S4 method for ANY compact_cols(x)  # S4 method for ANY compact_rows(x)

title: Remove Empty Rows/Columns — compact
text: x R object (matrix data.frame). ... Currently used. margin length-one numeric vector giving subscripts function applied (1 indicates rows, 2 indicates columns).

title: Remove Empty Rows/Columns — compact
text: row/column empty contains NA, zeros (type numeric) zero length character strings (type character).

title: Remove Empty Rows/Columns — compact
text: N. Frerebeau

title: Remove Empty Rows/Columns — compact
code: ## Create a data.frame X <- data.frame(A = 0, B = 1:5, C = 6, D = "", F = letters[1:5]) X #>   A B C D F #> 1 0 1 6   a #> 2 0 2 6   b #> 3 0 3 6   c #> 4 0 4 6   d #> 5 0 5 6   e  ## Remove empty columns compact(X, margin = 2) #>   B C F #> 1 1 6 a #> 2 2 6 b #> 3 3 6 c #> 4 4 6 d #> 5 5 6 e

title: Conditions — conditions
text: throw_error() stops execution current expression executes error action. throw_warning() generates warning message. catch_conditions() catch_message() handles unusual conditions.

title: Conditions — conditions
code: throw_error(.subclass, message, call = NULL, ...)  throw_warning(.subclass, message, call = NULL, ...)  catch_message(expr)  catch_conditions(expr)

title: Conditions — conditions
text: .subclass character string specifying class message returned. message character string specifying message returned. call call. ... Extra arguments. expr expression evaluated.

title: Conditions — conditions
text: N. Frerebeau

what: Confidence Interval for Binomial Proportions — confidence_binomial
title: Confidence Interval for Binomial Proportions — confidence_binomial
text: Computes Wald interval proportion desired level significance.

title: Confidence Interval for Binomial Proportions — confidence_binomial
code: confidence_binomial(object, ...)  # S4 method for numeric confidence_binomial(   object,   n,   level = 0.95,   method = "wald",   corrected = FALSE )

title: Confidence Interval for Binomial Proportions — confidence_binomial
text: object numeric vector giving number success. ... Currently used. n length-one numeric vector giving number trials. level length-one numeric vector giving confidence level. Must single number \(0\) \(1\). method character string specifying method used. unambiguous substring can used. corrected logical scalar: continuity correction used? used method "wald".

title: Confidence Interval for Binomial Proportions — confidence_binomial
text: length-two numeric vector giving lower upper confidence limits.

title: Confidence Interval for Binomial Proportions — confidence_binomial
text: N. Frerebeau

title: Confidence Interval for Binomial Proportions — confidence_binomial
code: ## Confidence interval for a mean x <- seq(from = -4, to = 4, by = 0.01) y <- dnorm(x)  confidence_mean(y, type = "student") #>     lower     upper  #> 0.1151118 0.1345606  confidence_mean(y, type = "normal") #>     lower     upper  #> 0.1151265 0.1345459   ## Confidence interval for a propotion confidence_binomial(118, n = 236) #>     lower     upper  #> 0.4362086 0.5637914   x <- c(35, 74, 22, 69) confidence_multinomial(x) #>           lower     upper #> [1,] 0.12234021 0.2276598 #> [2,] 0.30308797 0.4369120 #> [3,] 0.06663649 0.1533635 #> [4,] 0.27911853 0.4108815

what: Confidence Interval for a Mean — confidence_mean
title: Confidence Interval for a Mean — confidence_mean
text: Computes confidence interval mean desired level significance.

title: Confidence Interval for a Mean — confidence_mean
code: confidence_mean(object, ...)  # S4 method for numeric confidence_mean(object, level = 0.95, type = c("student", "normal"))

title: Confidence Interval for a Mean — confidence_mean
text: object numeric vector. ... Currently used. level length-one numeric vector giving confidence level. Must single number \(0\) \(1\). type character string giving type confidence interval returned. must one "student" (default) "normal". unambiguous substring can given.

title: Confidence Interval for a Mean — confidence_mean
text: length-two numeric vector giving lower upper confidence limits.

code: ## Confidence interval for a mean x <- seq(from = -4, to = 4, by = 0.01) y <- dnorm(x)  confidence_mean(y, type = "student") #>     lower     upper  #> 0.1151118 0.1345606  confidence_mean(y, type = "normal") #>     lower     upper  #> 0.1151265 0.1345459   ## Confidence interval for a propotion confidence_binomial(118, n = 236) #>     lower     upper  #> 0.4362086 0.5637914   x <- c(35, 74, 22, 69) confidence_multinomial(x) #>           lower     upper #> [1,] 0.12234021 0.2276598 #> [2,] 0.30308797 0.4369120 #> [3,] 0.06663649 0.1533635 #> [4,] 0.27911853 0.4108815

title: Confidence Interval for Multinomial Proportions — confidence_multinomial
code: confidence_multinomial(object, ...)  # S4 method for numeric confidence_multinomial(   object,   level = 0.95,   method = "wald",   corrected = FALSE )

title: Confidence Interval for Multinomial Proportions — confidence_multinomial
text: object numeric vector positive integers giving number occurrences class. ... Currently used. level length-one numeric vector giving confidence level. Must single number \(0\) \(1\). method character string specifying method used. unambiguous substring can used. corrected logical scalar: continuity correction used? used method "wald".

title: Confidence Interval for Multinomial Proportions — confidence_multinomial
text: two column numeric  matrix giving lower upper confidence limits.

title: Confidence Interval for Multinomial Proportions — confidence_multinomial
text: N. Frerebeau

title: Confidence Interval for Multinomial Proportions — confidence_multinomial
code: ## Confidence interval for a mean x <- seq(from = -4, to = 4, by = 0.01) y <- dnorm(x)  confidence_mean(y, type = "student") #>     lower     upper  #> 0.1151118 0.1345606  confidence_mean(y, type = "normal") #>     lower     upper  #> 0.1151265 0.1345459   ## Confidence interval for a propotion confidence_binomial(118, n = 236) #>     lower     upper  #> 0.4362086 0.5637914   x <- c(35, 74, 22, 69) confidence_multinomial(x) #>           lower     upper #> [1,] 0.12234021 0.2276598 #> [2,] 0.30308797 0.4369120 #> [3,] 0.06663649 0.1533635 #> [4,] 0.27911853 0.4108815

what: Count Values Using a Predicate — count
title: Count Values Using a Predicate — count
text: Counts values rows/columns using predicate function.

title: Count Values Using a Predicate — count
code: count(x, ...)  # S4 method for data.frame count(x, f, margin = 1, negate = FALSE, na.rm = FALSE, ...)  # S4 method for matrix count(x, f, margin = 1, negate = FALSE, na.rm = FALSE, ...)

title: Count Values Using a Predicate — count
text: x R object (matrix data.frame). ... arguments passed f. f predicate function. margin length-one numeric vector giving subscripts function applied (1 indicates rows, 2 indicates columns). negate logical scalar: negation f used instead f? na.rm logical scalar: NA values stripped computation proceeds?

title: Count Values Using a Predicate — count
text: numeric vector.

title: Count Values Using a Predicate — count
text: N. Frerebeau

title: Count Values Using a Predicate — count
code: ## Create a data matrix X <- matrix(sample(1:10, 25, TRUE), nrow = 5, ncol = 5)  ## Add NA k <- sample(1:25, 3, FALSE) X[k] <- NA X #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,]    3    2    6    6    7 #> [2,]    9    4    4   10    6 #> [3,]   10   10   10   NA   NA #> [4,]    1    8    4    8    7 #> [5,]    4    2   NA    9    5  ## Count missing values in rows count(X, f = is.na, margin = 1) #> [1] 0 0 2 0 1 ## Count non-missing values in columns count(X, f = is.na, margin = 2, negate = TRUE) #> V1 V2 V3 V4 V5  #>  5  5  4  4  4

what: Find Rows/Columns Using a Predicate — detect
title: Find Rows/Columns Using a Predicate — detect
text: Finds rows/columns array-like object using predicate function.

title: Find Rows/Columns Using a Predicate — detect
code: detect(x, ...)  # S4 method for ANY detect(x, f, margin = 1, negate = FALSE, all = FALSE, ...)

title: Find Rows/Columns Using a Predicate — detect
text: x R object (matrix data.frame). ... arguments passed f. f predicate function. margin length-one numeric vector giving subscripts function applied (1 indicates rows, 2 indicates columns). negate logical scalar: negation f used instead f? logical scalar. TRUE, rows/columns whose values meet condition defined f considered. FALSE (default), rows/columns least one value validates condition defined f considered.

title: Find Rows/Columns Using a Predicate — detect
text: logical vector.

title: Find Rows/Columns Using a Predicate — detect
text: N. Frerebeau

title: Find Rows/Columns Using a Predicate — detect
code: ## Create a data matrix X <- matrix(sample(1:10, 25, TRUE), nrow = 5, ncol = 5)  ## Add NA k <- sample(1:25, 3, FALSE) X[k] <- NA X #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,]    6   10    1    4    3 #> [2,]    1    7    1    9    3 #> [3,]    1    1    3    9    1 #> [4,]    7    8    5    1    5 #> [5,]   NA   NA    4   NA    9  ## Find row with NA detect(X, f = is.na, margin = 1) #> [1] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE  TRUE ## Find column without any NA detect(X, f = is.na, margin = 2, negate = TRUE, all = TRUE) #>    V1    V2    V3    V4    V5  #> FALSE FALSE  TRUE FALSE  TRUE

text: x R object (matrix data.frame). ... arguments passed f. f predicate function. margin length-one numeric vector giving subscripts function applied (1 indicates rows, 2 indicates columns). negate logical scalar: negation f used instead f? logical scalar. TRUE, rows/columns whose values meet condition defined f considered. FALSE (default), rows/columns least one value validates condition defined f considered. verbose logical scalar: R report extra information progress?

title: Remove Rows/Columns Using a Predicate — discard
text: N. Frerebeau

title: Remove Rows/Columns Using a Predicate — discard
code: ## Create a data matrix X <- matrix(sample(1:10, 25, TRUE), nrow = 5, ncol = 5)  ## Add NA k <- sample(1:25, 3, FALSE) X[k] <- NA X #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,]    3    6   NA    9    3 #> [2,]    2    5    5    8   NA #> [3,]    7    3    4    4    9 #> [4,]    1    4    5   NA    2 #> [5,]    8    5    1   10    5  ## Remove row with any NA discard(X, f = is.na, margin = 1, all = FALSE) #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,]    7    3    4    4    9 #> [2,]    8    5    1   10    5 ## Remove column with any NA discard(X, f = is.na, margin = 2, all = FALSE) #>      [,1] [,2] #> [1,]    3    6 #> [2,]    2    5 #> [3,]    7    3 #> [4,]    1    4 #> [5,]    8    5

what: Tools for Working With Empty String — empty
title: Tools for Working With Empty String — empty
text: remove_empty() remove rows/columns contain empty strings. replace_empty() replaces empty strings.

title: Tools for Working With Empty String — empty
code: remove_empty(x, ...)  replace_empty(x, ...)  # S4 method for ANY remove_empty(x, margin = 1, all = FALSE, na.rm = FALSE)  # S4 method for matrix replace_empty(x, value)  # S4 method for data.frame replace_empty(x, value)

title: Tools for Working With Empty String — empty
text: x R object (matrix data.frame). ... Currently used. margin length-one numeric vector giving subscripts function applied (1 indicates rows, 2 indicates columns). logical scalar. TRUE, rows/columns whose values meet condition defined f considered. FALSE (default), rows/columns least one value validates condition defined f considered. na.rm logical scalar: NA values stripped computation proceeds? value possible replacement value.

title: Tools for Working With Empty String — empty
text: N. Frerebeau

title: Tools for Working With Empty String — empty
code: ## Create a data matrix X <- matrix(sample(LETTERS, 25, TRUE), nrow = 5, ncol = 5)  ## Add empty string k <- sample(1:25, 3, FALSE) X[k] <- "" X #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,] "G"  "W"  ""   ""   "U"  #> [2,] "Y"  "X"  "U"  "V"  "N"  #> [3,] "X"  "X"  "I"  "E"  "D"  #> [4,] "T"  "Y"  "V"  "O"  "U"  #> [5,] "B"  ""   "A"  "Z"  "D"   ## Remove rows with empty strings remove_empty(X, margin = 1) #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,] "Y"  "X"  "U"  "V"  "N"  #> [2,] "X"  "X"  "I"  "E"  "D"  #> [3,] "T"  "Y"  "V"  "O"  "U"   ## Replace empty strings replace_empty(X, value = "XXX") #>      [,1] [,2]  [,3]  [,4]  [,5] #> [1,] "G"  "W"   "XXX" "XXX" "U"  #> [2,] "Y"  "X"   "U"   "V"   "N"  #> [3,] "X"  "X"   "I"   "E"   "D"  #> [4,] "T"  "Y"   "V"   "O"   "U"  #> [5,] "B"  "XXX" "A"   "Z"   "D"

what: Tools for Working With Infinite Values — infinite

title: Keep Rows/Columns Using a Predicate — keep
text: x R object (matrix data.frame). ... arguments passed f. f predicate function. margin length-one numeric vector giving subscripts function applied (1 indicates rows, 2 indicates columns). negate logical scalar: negation f used instead f? logical scalar. TRUE, rows/columns whose values meet condition defined f considered. FALSE (default), rows/columns least one value validates condition defined f considered. verbose logical scalar: R report extra information progress?

title: Keep Rows/Columns Using a Predicate — keep
text: N. Frerebeau

title: Keep Rows/Columns Using a Predicate — keep
code: ## Create a data matrix X <- matrix(sample(1:10, 25, TRUE), nrow = 5, ncol = 5)  ## Add NA k <- sample(1:25, 3, FALSE) X[k] <- NA X #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,]    2    5   10    6    2 #> [2,]   NA    6    2    2   NA #> [3,]    6    3    4    8    4 #> [4,]    3    5    3    6    2 #> [5,]    3   NA   10    9   10  ## Keep row without any NA keep(X, f = is.na, margin = 1, negate = TRUE, all = TRUE) #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] #> [1,]    2    5   10    6    2 #> [2,]    6    3    4    8    4 #> [3,]    3    5    3    6    2 ## Keep row without any NA keep(X, f = is.na, margin = 2, negate = TRUE, all = TRUE) #>      [,1] [,2] #> [1,]   10    6 #> [2,]    2    2 #> [3,]    4    8 #> [4,]    3    6 #> [5,]   10    9

title: Numeric Predicates — predicate-numeric
text: Check numeric objects: is_zero() checks object contains zeros. is_odd() is_even() check number odd even, respectively. is_positive() is_negative check object contains (strictly) positive negative numbers. is_whole() checks object contains whole numbers.

title: Numeric Predicates — predicate-numeric
code: is_zero(x, na.rm = FALSE)  is_odd(x, na.rm = FALSE)  is_even(x, na.rm = FALSE)  is_positive(x, strict = FALSE, na.rm = FALSE)  is_negative(x, strict = FALSE, na.rm = FALSE)  is_whole(x, na.rm = FALSE, tolerance = .Machine$double.eps^0.5)

title: Numeric Predicates — predicate-numeric
text: x numeric object tested. na.rm logical scalar: missing values (including NaN) omitted? strict logical scalar: strict inequality used? tolerance numeric scalar giving tolerance check within.

title: Numeric Predicates — predicate-numeric
text: logical vector.

code: ## Create a data.frame X <- data.frame(A = 1, B = 1:3) X #>   A B #> 1 1 1 #> 2 1 2 #> 3 1 3  remove_constant(X) #>   B #> 1 1 #> 2 2 #> 3 3  ## Add NA X[1, 1] <- NA remove_constant(X) #>    A B #> 1 NA 1 #> 2  1 2 #> 3  1 3 remove_constant(X, na.rm = TRUE) #>   B #> 1 1 #> 2 2 #> 3 3
